Article.ArticleTitle: Effects of caffeine intake and exercise intensity on executive and arousal vigilance.

Abstract.AbstractText: During physical efforts and sport practice, vigilance is responsible for maintaining an optimal state of activation, guaranteeing the ability to quickly respond and detect unexpected, but critical, stimuli over time. Caffeine and physical exercise are able to modulate the activation state, affecting vigilance performance. The aim of the present work was to assess the specific effects and modulations of caffeine intake and two physical intensities on vigilance components. Participants performed an attentional task (ANTI-Vea) to measure the executive and arousal components of vigilance, in six double-blinded counterbalanced sessions combining caffeine, placebo, or no-ingestion, with light vs. moderate cyclergometer exercise. Exercise at moderate intensity improved executive vigilance with faster overall reaction time (RT), without impairing error rates. Instead, caffeine intake generally improved arousal vigilance. In conclusion, caffeine and acute exercise seems to moderate executive and arousal vigilance in different ways.

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: Faculty of Physical Education & Sport Sciences, Catholic University of Valencia, Valencia, 46001, Spain. carlos.sanchis@ucv.es.

Department of Experimental Psychology, Mind, Brain and Behavior Research Center, University of Granada, 18071, Granada, Spain. jlupiane@ugr.es.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: The Catholic University of Valencia supported the treatment substances (caffeine and placebo) employed in this study as part of CS formative process. Additionally, the study was partially funded by the Ministerio de Economía y Competitividad, España (Ministry of Economy, Industry and Competitiveness, Spain) [grant number PSI2017-84926-P to JL] to the study of attention. The authors declare no additional competing or non-competing interests. The funders had no role in study design, experimental process, data collection and analysis or the manuscript preparation or submission process.
